question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where nonce[authorizer] has been advanced to type(uint256).max via N successful calls, causing the post-increment nonce[authorization.authorizer]++ to overflow to 0, permanently desyncing the nonce counter and making all future authorizations with nonce=0 appear valid, violating the invariant that nonce[authorizer] is strictly monotonically increasing? Proof idea: advance nonce[A] to type(uint256).max; submit Authorization{nonce=type(uint256).max}; assert nonce wraps to 0; assert future Authorization{nonce=0} is accepted.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker grief the authorizer by submitting a sequence of valid pre-signed authorizations (nonce=0, 1, 2, ..., N) in rapid succession, consuming all pre-signed nonces and forcing the authorizer to sign new authorizations, violating the invariant that a signed authorization can be submitted at the authorizer's chosen time? Proof idea: pre-sign N authorizations; attacker submits all N in one block; assert authorizer's intended authorization at nonce=K is consumed; assert authorizer must re-sign.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the signer is a delegate D who was authorized in Midnight AFTER the signature was created but BEFORE submission, and then D is revoked in Midnight BEFORE submission, causing the authorization to fail even though D was authorized at signing time, violating the invariant that signature validity is checked at submission time not signing time? Proof idea: A authorizes D; D signs Authorization; A revokes D; submit D's sig; assert Unauthorized revert because isAuthorized[A][D]=false at submission time.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the signer check (line 34: IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer)) reads Midnight state that was modified between the nonce check (line 26) and the signer check (line 34) within the same transaction via a reentrancy path, causing the authorization check to use post-modification state, violating the invariant that the signer authorization must be checked against the state at call entry? Proof idea: construct a callback that modifies isAuthorized state before line 34 executes; assert the check uses post-callback state; assert this can cause Unauthorized or unexpected acceptance.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == address(ecrecoverAuthorizer) and authorization.isAuthorized == false, causing EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke its own Midnight authorization for the authorizer and permanently disabling future signature-based authorizations for that authorizer, violating the invariant that EcrecoverAuthorizer's own Midnight authorization must not be self-revocable? Proof idea: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight; sign Authorization{authorized=ecrecoverAuthorizer, isAuthorized=false}; submit; assert isAuthorized[A][ecrecoverAuthorizer]=false; assert future EcrecoverAuthorizer calls for A revert at IMidnight.setIsAuthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == authorization.authorizer (self-authorization), causing isAuthorized[A][A]=true in Midnight, and verify whether this creates any unexpected behavior in Midnight's authorization checks where onBehalf==msg.sender is checked before isAuthorized, violating the invariant that self-authorization must be a no-op with no exploitable side effects? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=authorizer, isAuthorized=true}; submit; assert isAuthorized[A][A]=true; assert Midnight's onBehalf==msg.sender short-circuit still takes precedence in all functions.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized == address(0), causing isAuthorized[authorizer][address(0)]=true in Midnight, and verify whether any Midnight function uses address(0) as a valid operator in its authorization check, violating the invariant that address(0) must not be grantable as an authorized operator? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=address(0), isAuthorized=true}; submit; assert isAuthorized[A][address(0)]=true; check all Midnight functions for address(0) operator paths; assert no function accepts address(0) as msg.sender.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorized is the Midnight contract itself (authorized=MIDNIGHT), causing isAuthorized[authorizer][MIDNIGHT]=true, and verify whether Midnight can call its own functions on behalf of the authorizer, violating the invariant that the Midnight contract must not be grantable as an authorized operator of user accounts? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=MIDNIGHT}; submit; assert isAuthorized[A][MIDNIGHT]=true; check if any Midnight function can be triggered with msg.sender=MIDNIGHT on behalf of A.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where authorization.authorizer is a contract address (not an EOA), and ecrecover returns a non-zero address that happens to match that contract address due to a crafted (v, r, s), causing the contract to be treated as the signer, violating the invariant that ecrecover-based authorization is only valid for EOAs and contract addresses cannot sign? Proof idea: deploy a contract at a known address; craft (v, r, s) such that ecrecover returns that address; submit Authorization{authorizer=contractAddress}; assert InvalidSignature or Unauthorized; assert contract addresses cannot produce valid ECDSA signatures.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the permissionless nature of setIsAuthorized (no msg.sender restriction) to submit a valid authorization in the same Midnight multicall as other operations, applying the authorization mid-bundle to enable subsequent unauthorized operations within the same multicall, violating the invariant that authorization state changes must not enable unauthorized operations in the same multicall bundle? Proof idea: multicall [ecrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig), midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, 0, assets, A, attacker)]; assert withdrawCollateral fails if attacker is not authorized at call entry.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker observe that the SetIsAuthorized event (lines 38-44) emits authorization.nonce (the pre-increment value) while storage nonce[authorizer] is already incremented to nonce+1, and verify that off-chain indexers relying on the event nonce to reconstruct state are not confused or exploitable, violating the invariant that the emitted nonce must unambiguously correspond to the consumed nonce? Proof idea: submit valid Authorization{nonce=N}; assert event emits nonce=N; assert storage nonce[authorizer]=N+1; assert no off-chain replay is possible using the emitted nonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the Signature struct is declared as 'Signature memory' in the IEcrecoverAuthorizer interface (line 43) but as 'Signature calldata' in the EcrecoverAuthorizer implementation (line 24), and verify that this ABI encoding mismatch does not cause signature data corruption when called via the interface, violating the invariant that the interface and implementation must agree on parameter encoding? Proof idea: call setIsAuthorized via IEcrecoverAuthorizer interface (memory) vs direct call (calldata); assert identical (v, r, s) values are received; fuzz all (v, r, s) inputs.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit two Authorizations in the same block (same block.timestamp) where the first sets isAuthorized=true and the second sets isAuthorized=false for the same (authorizer, authorized) pair, and verify that the final state reflects the second authorization (last-write-wins), violating the invariant that the last submitted authorization in a block determines the final state and no intermediate state is exploitable? Proof idea: submit auth1{isAuthorized=true, nonce=0} then auth2{isAuthorized=false, nonce=1} in same block; assert isAuthorized[A][B]=false; assert no window exists between the two calls where B can exploit the true state.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the full downstream effect is: EcrecoverAuthorizer grants operator X access to A's account; X calls midnight.withdrawCollateral(market, index, assets, A, X) to drain A's collateral to X's address; violating the invariant that authorization granted via EcrecoverAuthorizer must not enable collateral withdrawal to an attacker-controlled address without the authorizer's explicit intent? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=X}; submit; X calls withdrawCollateral(market, 0, collateral[A], A, X); assert A's collateral is drained to X.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the full downstream effect is: EcrecoverAuthorizer grants operator X access to A's account; X calls midnight.setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, A) to cancel all of A's maker offers in a group; violating the invariant that authorization granted via EcrecoverAuthorizer must not enable offer cancellation without the authorizer's explicit intent? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=X}; submit; X calls setConsumed(group, type(uint256).max, A); assert A's offers in group are permanently cancelled.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the full downstream effect is: EcrecoverAuthorizer grants operator X access to A's account; X calls midnight.take(offer, ..., A, X, ...) to create debt for A while sending loan tokens to X; violating the invariant that authorization granted via EcrecoverAuthorizer must not enable unauthorized debt creation for the authorizer? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=X}; submit; X calls take(lenderOffer, hex'', units, A, X, address(0), hex''); assert debtOf(id, A) increases and X receives loan tokens.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the full downstream effect is: EcrecoverAuthorizer grants operator X access to A's account; X calls midnight.withdraw(market, units, A, X) to drain A's credit to X's address; violating the invariant that authorization granted via EcrecoverAuthorizer must not enable credit withdrawal to an attacker-controlled address? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=X}; submit; X calls withdraw(market, creditOf(id, A), A, X); assert A's credit is drained to X.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the authorized address is a flash loan callback contract, granting it operator access to A's account, and then use a flash loan to trigger the callback which calls Midnight functions on behalf of A, violating the invariant that flash loan callbacks must not gain unauthorized operator access to user accounts? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=flashLoanCallback}; submit; trigger midnight.flashLoan; assert callback can call withdrawCollateral on behalf of A; assert A's collateral is drained.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the authorized address is a Midnight ratifier contract, granting it operator access to A's account, and then use that ratifier's isRatified callback to call Midnight functions on behalf of A during a take, violating the invariant that ratifier callbacks must not gain unauthorized operator access to user accounts? Proof idea: sign Authorization{authorized=maliciousRatifier}; submit; trigger take with maliciousRatifier; assert ratifier's isRatified callback can call withdrawCollateral on behalf of A.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the signer D is a delegate authorized in Midnight, and D's authorization was itself granted via EcrecoverAuthorizer (not directly by A), creating a chain where EcrecoverAuthorizer's validity depends on state it previously set, and verify that this circular dependency does not create exploitable inconsistencies, violating the invariant that the authorization check must not depend on state created by the same contract in the same call chain? Proof idea: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer; ecrecoverAuthorizer grants D; D signs new Authorization; submit; assert D's signature is accepted; assert no reentrancy or state inconsistency.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where A revokes EcrecoverAuthorizer's Midnight access (isAuthorized[A][ecrecoverAuthorizer]=false) after granting delegate D access via EcrecoverAuthorizer, and then D attempts to sign a new Authorization for A, causing the IMidnight.setIsAuthorized call to revert because ecrecoverAuthorizer is no longer authorized, violating the invariant that revoking EcrecoverAuthorizer must prevent all future delegate-signed authorizations for that authorizer? Proof idea: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer; ecrecoverAuthorizer grants D; A revokes ecrecoverAuthorizer; D signs new Authorization; submit; assert IMidnight.setIsAuthorized reverts with Unauthorized.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).setIsAuthorized call (line 47) reverts because EcrecoverAuthorizer is not authorized in Midnight for the authorizer, and verify that the nonce is NOT incremented (full revert), violating the invariant that a failed IMidnight.setIsAuthorized call must not advance nonce[authorizer]? Proof idea: submit Authorization where isAuthorized[A][ecrecoverAuthorizer]=false; assert IMidnight.setIsAuthorized reverts; assert nonce[A] unchanged; assert pre-signed auth with same nonce is still valid.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the full call sequence is: (1) attacker observes A's signed Authorization{authorized=B, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0}; (2) attacker submits it; (3) attacker immediately submits Authorization{authorized=B, isAuthorized=false, nonce=1} signed by themselves as a delegate D; causing B's authorization to be toggled without A's knowledge, violating the invariant that the net authorization state must reflect the authorizer's most recent intent? Proof idea: submit auth1{isAuthorized=true, nonce=0} then auth2{isAuthorized=false, nonce=1} signed by D; assert final isAuthorized[A][B]=false; assert A's intent was isAuthorized=true.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the domain separator is computed dynamically on every call (not cached), and verify that after a hard fork where block.chainid changes, previously valid signatures are rejected and new signatures for the new chainId cannot be replayed on the old chain, violating the invariant that chain separation is enforced by the dynamic domain separator? Proof idea: sign with chainId=1; simulate fork to chainId=2; assert old sig rejected; sign with chainId=2; simulate revert to chainId=1; assert new sig rejected.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Signature replay, nonce desync, deadline edge case, or EIP-712 domain mistake that affects delegated authorization'] [Function: setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged attacker submit an Authorization where the nonce[authorizer] in EcrecoverAuthorizer is desynchronized from the authorization state in Midnight (e.g., A revokes ecrecoverAuthorizer, nonce advances, A re-grants ecrecoverAuthorizer), causing pre-signed authorizations with old nonces to be permanently invalid even though A re-granted access, violating the invariant that re-granting EcrecoverAuthorizer access must restore the ability to use pre-signed authorizations? Proof idea: A authorizes ecrecoverAuthorizer; advance nonce to N; A revokes ecrecoverAuthorizer; A re-grants ecrecoverAuthorizer; assert pre-signed auth at nonce=0 is permanently invalid; assert only auth at nonce=N is valid.
